note: CANONICAL source-of-truth for the three-layer taxonomy the whole Sonaloop product aligns on (core app, website IA, tracker). The human-readable companion is docs/job-framework-format.md — read that first. Jobs reference REAL methodology keys under sonaloop/methodologies/*.json and format ids that are implemented or planned. Stable ids/labels here are what every repo and ticket tags itself with.

job.name: Job
job.one_liner: What the user wants — the use case / solution they buy.
job.definition: The outcome a customer walks in for ("how is my positioning?", "what should I charge?"). It is the thing sold and the thing measured. The core has no first-class concept of it today — the host (Claude) improvises it from a free-text goal; this taxonomy gives Jobs stable ids so presets, the website, and the plan engine can name them.
job.doctor_analogy: The Job is WHY you walked in: "why does my stomach hurt?"

framework.name: Framework
framework.one_liner: The process the run follows end-to-end.
framework.definition: A constellation of steps (a DAG) that takes a run from ambiguity to a buildable answer — Double Diamond, Double Diamond Deep, d.school Micro-Cycle, Lean/JTBD. Frameworks already exist in core as sonaloop/methodologies/*.json (keyed by the `key` field) and seed the plan engine.
framework.doctor_analogy: The Framework is the doctor's PROCESS: history -> exam -> tests -> diagnose.

format.name: Format
format.one_liner: A single move inside a run.
format.definition: One concrete research move executed at a plan step: a council, a prototype test, a head-to-head, a red-team. Formats are orthogonal to Jobs and Frameworks — the same Format appears inside many Frameworks serving many Jobs.
format.doctor_analogy: The Format is ONE test: a blood test, an X-ray. The doctor reaches for Formats inside the process.

formats.name: Council
formats.definition: Several segment-diverse personas react to a prompt and to each other in one session — the core diverge move.

formats.name: Prototype Test
formats.definition: Personas actually USE a prototype (a real Playwright-harness session) and react, grounding the convergence.

formats.name: Head-to-Head
formats.definition: Personas compare two or more concrete options (captured A/B variants or plain text options) side-by-side and force a preference; the server tallies a reasoned, segmented preference (preference + margin + segment-splits). Run via the head_to_head Format (brief_head_to_head / record_head_to_head).

formats.name: Red-Team
formats.definition: A falsification council that deliberately argues the NEGATIVE case — why a segment would NOT adopt / NOT pay / would churn — to stress-test the idea instead of flattering it. Each persona gets an explicit adversarial lens (skeptic / blocker / switching-cost / status-quo / risk); the server groups their concrete objections by theme into a structured case-against (blockers per persona + worst severity), optionally beside the case-for. Run via the red_team Format (brief_red_team / record_red_team).

jobs.name: Positioning
jobs.user_question: How is my positioning — does my value land, and against whom?

coverage.note: Cover both your target segment and the segments you are NOT for, plus at least one user of the incumbent alternative, so the positioning is tested against real competition.

jobs.name: Pricing / Price-Sensitivity
jobs.user_question: What should I charge, and where does price sensitivity break?

coverage.note: Span low-to-high willingness-to-pay and include the budget holder; a head-to-head on price points plus a red-team of the value story exposes the sensitivity curve.

protocol.name: Willingness-to-pay ladder
protocol.summary: Van-Westendorp-style price ladder: a fixed ascending ladder of price points, each persona reacting to EVERY point with one of four anchor bands (too_cheap / bargain / getting_expensive / too_expensive) grounded in their profile budget/constraints; the server derives the acceptable-price range and the cliff point per segment; tier comparisons reuse head_to_head with price as variant metadata.

steps.rule: The ladder — at least two ascending price points — is fixed BEFORE any persona reacts; a changed ladder is a new run.

Each persona reacts to EACH price point with exactly ONE anchor band — too_cheap (suspiciously cheap), bargain, getting_expensive, too_expensive — plus a short grounding quote; off-vocabulary bands are rejected.
Reactions are grounded in the persona's budget, authority and constraints from agent_context — quote them, never invent a wallet.
steps.tooling: brief_price_ladder participants[].agent_context (profile + budget signals folded in)

steps.rule: The server derives the acceptable-price range (where a majority sits in bargain/getting_expensive) and the cliff point (the largest acceptance drop between adjacent rungs) — overall AND per segment; the host argues the pricing story.
Tier/packaging comparisons are a HEAD-TO-HEAD with price as variant metadata, not a second ladder — the ladder finds the range, the head-to-head picks the tier.

jobs.name: Demand / Jobs-to-be-Done
jobs.user_question: What job are customers hiring this for, and is there real demand?

coverage.note: "JTBD" is a JOB whose core engine is the lean_jtbd FRAMEWORK of the same name — keep the two layers distinct. Cover distinct trigger moments and the alternatives customers hire today.

jobs.name: Ideation (How-Might-We)
jobs.user_question: Given this How-Might-We, what could we build?

coverage.note: How-Might-We is really a FORMAT-shaped move often mis-sold as a Job; here it is the ideation Job. Include at least one extreme user to push non-obvious concepts.

jobs.name: Continuous Discovery
jobs.user_question: What should we learn next, on a steady cadence?

coverage.note: A light, repeatable loop rather than a one-off. Keep a small standing panel that spans lifecycle stages so signal is comparable cadence-over-cadence.

jobs.name: Churn Reasons
jobs.user_question: Why are customers leaving, and which reasons are addressable?

coverage.note: Fits naturally as a focused continuous-discovery Job. Cover distinct churn reasons and where they switched to; red-team your retention story against them.

jobs.name: A/B Test
jobs.user_question: Which variant wins — and for whom?

coverage.note: Segment-DIVERSE by design: the verdict is segmented (overall + per-segment winner), so the panel must span every segment the variants could split. Four personas minimum, no segment owning the panel.

protocol.name: A/B test discipline
protocol.summary: What separates an A/B test from a vibe-check: variants frozen up front, ONE hypothesis + success metric stamped before exposure, per-persona randomized variant order (position-bias guard), a forced preference with intensity from every persona, and a segmented verdict (overall + per-segment winner, margin, abstentions).

steps.rule: All variants are defined and FROZEN before any persona sees them — never edit an option mid-run; a changed variant is a new test.
steps.tooling: add_artifact (captured A/B variants) or plain text options on brief_head_to_head / record_head_to_head

steps.rule: Exactly ONE falsifiable hypothesis + success metric is stamped BEFORE exposure, and the recording carries its ref — no post-hoc rationalisation.
steps.tooling: record_hypothesis → pass its id as variant_meta.hypothesis_id to record_head_to_head

steps.rule: Variant presentation order is randomized PER PERSONA (position-bias guard) and the order actually shown is recorded with the result.
Every persona states a FORCED preference plus intensity (per-option stance -2..2 and one choice with a reason). A genuine 'torn' is an abstention and is counted as one, never silently dropped.
steps.tooling: record_head_to_head preferences=[{persona_id, choice, intensity?, reason}] + per-option statements

steps.rule: The verdict is overall AND per-segment — winner, margin, abstentions per segment — never a single blended number that hides who the loser variant still wins.
